# kotlin-itertools

> Multiplatform iterator tools and combinatoric sequences for Kotlin.

Kotlin Multiplatform library providing lazy `Sequence`-based iterator tools and combinatoric functions, inspired by Python's itertools.

Published to Maven Central as `com.michael-bull.kotlin-itertools`.

## Full Documentation

- [llms-full.txt](https://github.com/michaelbull/kotlin-itertools/blob/master/llms-full.txt): Comprehensive API reference with signatures, descriptions, and examples — self-contained, no web fetches required

## Installation

```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation("com.michael-bull.kotlin-itertools:kotlin-itertools:1.1.0")
}
```

## API Overview

All functions are extension functions that return lazy `Sequence<T>` types.

### Iterator Tools

- `Iterable<T>.cycle()` / `cycle(times)` — infinite or bounded cycling of elements
- `Iterable<T>.interleave(other)` / `Sequence<T>.interleave(other)` — alternate elements from two iterables, stopping at the shorter
- `Iterable<T>.interleaveLongest(other)` / `Sequence<T>.interleaveLongest(other)` — alternate elements, continuing until both exhausted
- `Iterable<T>.intersperse(separator)` / `Sequence<T>.intersperse(separator)` — insert separator between adjacent elements

### Combinatoric Sequences

- `List<T>.combinations(k)` / `pairCombinations()` / `tripleCombinations()` — k-sized combinations
- `List<T>.combinationsWithReplacement(k)` / `pairCombinationsWithReplacement()` / `tripleCombinationsWithReplacement()` — combinations allowing repeated elements
- `List<T>.powerset()` — all subsets from empty to full
- `List<T>.permutations(k)` / `pairPermutations()` / `triplePermutations()` — k-sized permutations
- `List<T>.distinctPermutations()` — permutations with value-based uniqueness
- `List<T>.permutationsWithReplacement(k)` / `pairPermutationsWithReplacement()` / `triplePermutationsWithReplacement()` — permutations allowing repeated elements
- `List<T>.derangements(k)` — permutations where no element stays in its original position
- `Iterable<A>.product(other)` / `product(first, second)` / `List<List<T>>.product()` — Cartesian product
